channel.title: NYT > Style
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: In ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2,’ Caleb Hearon Gets the Job ‘a Million Girls Would Kill For’
item.description: Caleb Hearon is circling the mainstream with a role in “The Devil Wears Prada 2,” but does his podcast-friendly personality translate into Hollywood stardom?
item.dc:creator: Sandra E. Garcia
item.media:credit: Brian Karlsson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Caleb Hearon, a comedian whose Midwestern affability draws people in, joined the world of high fashion in “The Devil Wears Prada 2.”

item.title: Can They Build ‘America First’ Without Nick Fuentes?
item.description: A small group of candidates and influencers met to forge a shaky new political movement — without its de facto leader and his groypers.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Taylor Pemberton
item.media:credit: Brian Kaiser for The New York Times
item.media:description: On a Saturday afternoon in May, an eclectic group that included Casey Putsch, a Republican running for governor in Ohio, tried to find common ground under the America First banner.

item.title: The Many Love Lives of Ted Turner
item.description: Known as a playboy, the media mogul gave his paramours and three ex-wives plenty of stories to tell. He also managed to stay friendly with many of them.
item.dc:creator: Sadiba Hasan
item.media:credit: Ron Galella Collection, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Jane Fonda, the Academy Award-winning actress, wrote in her memoir that Turner cheated on her a month after they married.

item.title: How Heidi Klum Got Her 2026 Met Gala Statue Look
item.description: “I could eat and use my hands quite comfortably,” Klum said of her intricate Roman statue ensemble, which was inspired by a marble sculpture by Raffaele Monti.
item.dc:creator: Alisha Haridasani Gupta
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Heidi Klum arrived at the Met Gala, which had the theme “Fashion Is Art,” dressed as a marble statue.

item.title: Need to Catch Up on the Met Gala? Read This.
item.description: We’ve got your morning-after recap.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Gallagher
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times

item.title: Having Trouble Choosing the Right White for Your Wedding? This Color Analyst Can Help.
item.description: Megan Bentley, a color analyst, shares how identifying your color season, the hues that are most complementary or flattering, can elevate your wedding wardrobe.
item.dc:creator: Ivy Manners
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Megan Bentley, left, a color analyst and the founder of The Color Countess, based in Columbus, Ohio, works with clients to identify their most flattering color palettes.

item.title: Was It Art? Was It Fashion? Was It Good?
item.description: Guests at the Met Gala had different interpretations of the night’s dress code.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kim Kardashian, in an orange fiberglass breastplate by the British pop artist Allen Jones.

item.title: Inside the Met Gala After-Parties: See the Stars and Their Looks
item.description: Models, designers, D.J.s and performers kept the night going into the early morning.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Bernstein and Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Rebecca Smeyne for The New York Times

item.title: Why Everyone Was So Mad About the Met Gala
item.description: Plus, a remembrance of Ted Turner and a tank top quandary.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Gallagher
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Beyoncé arriving at the Met Gala on Monday.

item.title: What Is Body Dysmorphic Disorder?
item.description: It may look like vanity, but it’s a debilitating mental health condition.
item.dc:creator: Christina Caron

item.title: Writing About My Mother as if She Were Dead
item.description: I needed distance to write honestly about my childhood. But fiction didn’t protect any of us from pain.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Landis
item.media:credit: Brian Rea

item.title: They Met One Night in Queens. A Year in St. Lucia Followed.
item.description: A chance meeting in Queens led Shanae Strachan and Lance Growth to build a life between New York and St. Lucia before marrying in Manhattan.
item.dc:creator: Donna Kim

item.title: Unmistakable Love of Austin, the Texas Longhorns and Each Other
item.description: Adam Mena and Jesse Stowell brought Austin, Texas, alive at their wedding with a taco truck, burnt orange hues and two miniature shaggy Highland cows.
item.dc:creator: Rosalie R. Radomsky

item.title: She Had Seen Her in Photos. Then They Met in Real Life.
item.description: Nandika Chatterjee had heard about Abigail Braggins from a roommate and thought she was “cute.” A wave in Little Italy sealed their fate.
item.dc:creator: Stella Raine Chu

item.title: A Second Chance Led Them Back to Each Other
item.description: When Matt Sornson and Negarin Sadr first met, they didn’t quite hit it off. But after reconnecting years later online, they built a strong foundation that would later be tested.
item.dc:creator: Sadiba Hasan

item.title: In Cartagena, a Place for Monks and Movie Lovers Is Now a Hotel
item.description: In Cartagena, Colombia, historic buildings, including a monastery and a nightclub, were transformed into a Four Seasons.
item.dc:creator: Ray Mark Rinaldi

item.title: This Film Provides a Trapezoidal Window into Former Pizza Huts
item.description: A documentary about the popular chain presents its repurposed buildings as protective shells where new businesses can grow.
item.dc:creator: Steven Kurutz
item.media:credit: Clark Hodgin for The New York Times
item.media:description: The filmmakers Matthew Salleh and Rose Tucker at a Pizza Hut in Newburgh, N.Y. Their documentary “Slice of Life: The American Dream. In Former Pizza Huts.” came out last year.

item.title: A Fashion Revolution at the Met
item.description: With “Costume Art,” the dress department moves out of the basement to become the gateway to the museum.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman and Marcus Maddox
item.media:description: Unexpected bedfellows: The entry room of “Costume Art,” focusing on the naked and nude body, sets the tone for the show, with every garment paired with an accompanying piece from around the museum.

item.title: I’m Unhappily Single. Do I Have to Attend My Friend’s Wedding?
item.description: Our Ask the Therapist columnist, Lori Gottlieb, advises a reader who doesn’t want to be reminded of her loneliness yet again.
item.dc:creator: Lori Gottlieb
item.media:credit: Marta Monteiro

item.title: Former Amazon Union Leader Who Was Arrested at Met Gala Has No Regrets
item.description: Christian Smalls, a longtime Amazon antagonist, was charged with trespassing and another misdemeanor, and spent 24 hours in jail. The union disavowed his actions.
item.dc:creator: Jesse McKinley
item.media:credit: Brendan Mcdermid/Reuters
item.media:description: Mr. Smalls had led a successful effort to unionize an Amazon warehouse on Staten Island in 2022.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Some Delight to Your Day
item.description: Buried treasure, birds on the move and secret library access.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Tiny Love Stories: ‘I Could Feel Him Watching Me’
item.description: Modern Love in miniature, featuring reader-submitted stories of no more than 100 words.
item.media:credit: Brian Rea

item.title: A Southern Magazine Now Calls a Historic Charleston Jail Home
item.description: Based in Charleston, S.C., Garden & Gun recently moved to a restored landmark on Magazine Street, of all places.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Hunt and Hunter Mcrae For The New York Times
item.media:credit: Hunter McRae for The New York Times
item.media:description: The “study” area of the new headquarters of Garden & Gun magazine, which moved into Charleston’s Old Jail in March.

item.title: My Mother Won’t Reschedule Her Elective Surgery So I Can Be There. Help!
item.description: A reader wants her mother to pick another date for a hip-replacement surgery so that she doesn’t have to miss an important work conference. Cue the cutting email from Mom.
item.dc:creator: Philip Galanes
item.media:credit: Miguel Porlan

item.title: For an Indiana Couple, This Schoolhouse Became Their Home
item.description: Stacie Grissom and Sean Wilson’s 9,000-square-foot Indiana schoolhouse is a lesson in courage.
item.dc:creator: Lia Picard and Michelle Litvin
item.media:description: Stacie Grissom and Sean Wilson with their children. The couple renovated the former Union Joint schoolhouse in Franklin, Ind., with a sense of whimsy.

item.title: Street Style Look of the Week: A Brand Loyalist Steps Out in Blue
item.description: A boutique owner with a closet bursting with Ralph Lauren stepped out in blue.
item.dc:creator: Simbarashe Cha

item.title: Donlyn Lyndon, Last Surviving Creator of the Sea Ranch, Dies at 90
item.description: With his classmates Charles Moore, William Turnbull Jr. and Richard Whitaker, he designed an icon of modern architecture on a windswept bluff in Northern California.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: Mingling With Jeff Bezos and Stevie Nicks at the 2026 Met Gala Cocktail Party
item.description: An exclusive glimpse inside the party, where guests had many interpretations for the “Fashion Is Art” dress code.
item.dc:creator: Sandra E. Garcia and Landon Nordeman
item.media:description: Blue Ivy Carter and Beyoncé making their entrance.

item.title: Our 15 Favorite Looks at the 2026 Met Gala
item.description: Regardless of the theme, these people just looked great.
item.dc:creator: The Styles Desk
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times

item.title: Kylie Jenner’s Nipple-Forward Look Pointed to a Met Gala Red Carpet Trend
item.description: Nipples both faux and flesh were abundant on the Met Gala carpet.
item.dc:creator: Alisha Haridasani Gupta
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kylie Jenner posed in a custom Schiaparelli gown.

item.title: Connor Storrie and Alysa Liu Are Among the First-Time Met Gala Attendees
item.description: Some of those making their Met debuts also happened to have the evening’s most arresting looks.
item.dc:creator: Yola Mzizi
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Connor Storrie of “Heated Rivalry” at the Met Gala.

item.title: No Reverse-Aging for Bad Bunny at the Met Gala
item.description: The singer, 32, said it took “53 years” to prepare his look.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Taylor Pemberton
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bad Bunny opted to age himself up, while many other guests worked to look younger.

item.title: Jeff Bezos and Mark Zuckerberg Were Notably Missing From the Met Gala Carpet
item.description: No Bezos. No Zuckerberg.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Gallagher
item.media:credit: Landon Nordeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jeff Bezos saved his socializing for inside the event.

item.title: Heidi Klum turned to stone for her Met Gala look.
item.description: The model’s trompe l’oeil outfit would have been equally at home at one of her Halloween parties.
item.dc:creator: Alisha Haridasani Gupta
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times

item.title: Sabrina Carpenter Wears ‘Sabrina’ Film Dress to the Met Gala
item.description: It was perhaps the Met Gala’s most meta moment.
item.dc:creator: Jesse McKinley
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Sabrina” on Sabrina.

item.title: Some New York City Democrats Went to the Met Gala
item.description: Mayor Mamdani sat out the fund-raiser. But a number of Democratic city politicians, past and present attended the extravagant affair.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Taylor Pemberton
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nantasha Williams, the deputy speaker for New York’s City Council, arrives at the Met Gala.

item.title: At the Met Gala, Hands and Arms Appear on the Red Carpet
item.description: Lisa wasn’t the only celebrity to show up at the Met Gala with extra appendages.
item.dc:creator: Gina Cherelus
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jordan Roth, the Tony Award-winning producer and performer, wore a velvet gown with a figure resembling a conjoined twin peeking out from behind.

item.title: Beyoncé, Blue Ivy and Jay-Z Arrive at the 2026 Met Gala
item.description: The superstar, a co-chair of this year’s gala, electrified onlookers when she arrived with her elder daughter and husband.
item.dc:creator: Melina Delkic
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Beyoncé arrives at the Met Gala.

item.title: Zendaya, Bella Hadid and Other Stars Rumored to Skip the Met Gala
item.description: Some luminaries had scheduling conflicts, but there were some who chose not to attend to make a statement.
item.dc:creator: Alisha Haridasani Gupta
item.media:credit: Monica Schipper/Getty Images; Scott A Garfitt/Invision, via Associated Press

item.title: Google Founder Sergey Brin Hits the Met Gala’s Red Carpet
item.description: Sergey Brin, a Google founder, in a dark tuxedo alongside his girlfriend, a conservative influencer.
item.dc:creator: Jesse McKinley
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sergey Brin and Gerelyn Gilbert at the 2026 Met Gala in Manhattan.

item.title: Mayor Mamdani Highlights Fashion Workers as He Skips Met Gala
item.description: New York’s mayor highlighted the seamstresses, tailors and other workers in the fashion industry.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Gallagher
item.media:credit: Madison Swart for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani in New York, last month.

item.title: Mark Zuckerberg Quietly Attends His First Met Gala
item.description: The former nerd completes his fashion Met-amorphosis.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman

item.title: An N.B.A. Upset Forces a Showdown in Manhattan: Met Gala or Knicks Game?
item.description: A home playoff game on Monday night may pose an issue for Knicks superfans like Spike Lee and Timothée Chalamet.
item.dc:creator: Callie Holtermann
item.media:credit: Sarah Stier/Getty Images
item.media:description: From left, Tina Fey, Timothée Chalamet, Kylie Jenner, Ben Stiller and Christine Taylor at a New York Knicks playoff game on Tuesday.

item.title: Met Gala 2026 Red Carpet Photos: All the Looks
item.description: See the arrivals on the cobblestone carpet.
item.dc:creator: The Styles Desk, Nina Westervelt and Amir Hamja
item.media:credit: Jamie Mccarthy/Getty Images

item.title: In Beyoncé’s Met Gala appearance, fans see potential for a big announcement.
item.dc:creator: Gina Cherelus
item.media:credit: Josh Haner/The New York Times
item.media:description: Beyoncé at the Met Gala in 2015.

item.title: A ‘Ball Without Billionaires’ Kicks Off the First Monday in May
item.description: Labor unions from around the country staged a different kind of fashion show in downtown Manhattan.
item.dc:creator: Alisha Haridasani Gupta
item.media:credit: via the Service Employees International Union
item.media:description: Former Washington Post staffers at the 2026 Ball Without Billionaires.

item.title: Understanding the ‘body types’ on display at this year’s fashion exhibition.
item.dc:creator: Callie Holtermann
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: A corset ensemble by the designer Michaela Stark, fitted on a mannequin made in her image.

item.title: The Met opens its fashion exhibition with big names and a new fund-raising record.
item.dc:creator: Gina Cherelus
item.media:credit: Roy Rochlin/Getty Images
item.media:description: The morning of the Met Gala, the museum unveiled its corresponding fashion exhibition, “Costume Art,” in the Costume Institute’s new gallery space, formerly the gift shop.

item.title: Many firsts on the first Monday in May.
item.dc:creator: The Styles Desk

item.title: Doris Fisher, Co-Founder of the Gap, Dies at 94
item.description: The company she started in 1969 with her husband, Don, grew from a single store selling jeans and records to a $16 billion brand that remade the apparel industry.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Rifkin
item.media:credit: Pool photo/Hector Amezcua, via Reuters
item.media:description: Doris Fisher in 2011, when she was inducted into the California Hall of Fame along with her late husband, Don.

item.title: The Met Gala’s Embrace of Jeff Bezos Causes a Backlash
item.description: Protesters have found a perfect foil in Amazon’s founder, the gala’s lead sponsor this year.
item.dc:creator: Jesse McKinley and Alisha Haridasani Gupta
item.media:credit: Shannon Stapleton/Reuters
item.media:description: In the weeks leading up to the event, an increasingly visible anti-Bezos campaign has erupted on New York’s streets and subways. On social media, users have described the event as the “Amazon Prime Gala” or “Bezos Ball.”

item.title: The Met Makes a Statement With 9 New Mannequin Bodies
item.description: The latest Costume Institute exhibition expands its ideas of who, exactly, belongs in fashion. Will the gala follow suit?
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Paul Westlake, via The Metropolitan Museum of Art
item.media:description: Five of the new mannequins in the new Costume Institute exhibition, each of which have been modeled on actual people.

item.title: Why Are So Many Men Wearing Tank Tops?
item.description: A reader wonders why the ribbed undershirt has become the ubiquitous garment of the moment.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman
item.media:credit: Jeremy Moeller/Getty Images; Edward Berthelot/Getty Images; Broadimage/Shutterstock
item.media:description: Clockwise from left: the soccer player Jean-Claude Mpassy, a guest at Milan Fashion Week and Justin Bieber.

item.title: Vogue Alumni Stage a Reunion for ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’
item.description: A gaggle of former and current Vogue employees convened for a Saturday night screening of “The Devil Wears Prada 2.” The dress code for the evening? Interview chic.
item.dc:creator: Sandra E. Garcia
item.media:credit: Emma Rose Milligan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Vogue alumnae caught an opening-weekend showing of “The Devil Wears Prada 2” in Battery Park City in Manhattan on Saturday.

item.title: Scarlet Oak, Where D.C.’s Young Conservatives Go Out to Play
item.description: Scarlet Oak, a restaurant and bar in the Navy Yard section of Washington, has become a hangout for a younger MAGA cohort. Especially on Wine Wednesdays.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan
item.media:credit: Pete Kiehart for The New York Times
item.media:description: On Wednesday evenings, most bottles of wine at Scarlet Oak are half-price, drawing a young crowd.

item.title: The First Lady of Kentucky on Dressing for the Derby — and the Spotlight
item.description: The first lady of Kentucky talks hat dos and don’ts, the best way to walk across a horse track and balancing personal and political style.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman and Madeleine Hordinski
